content:
{
  "title": "Defensive Contextual Prompting: Adaptive Guardrails for Robust LLM Response",
  "problem": "Large language models are vulnerable to adversarial prompts and jailbreak attacks that bypass safety filters and elicit harmful or restricted outputs. Existing static prompt engineering or system instructions often fail to generalize to novel adversarial strategies.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive applications, ensuring their robustness against sophisticated adversarial inputs is critical for user safety and regulatory compliance. Recent work shows attackers can exploit prompt variations and context injection to evade fixed defenses, exposing a need for adaptive, context-aware defense mechanisms.",
  "method": "We propose Defensive Contextual Prompting (DCP), a method that dynamically augments user prompts with adversarially-trained guardrail tokens and meta-context derived from real-time analysis of the input. Given an incoming prompt, a lightweight classifier first predicts its adversarial likelihood. If suspicious, a generator module appends context-specific defensive instructions (e.g., explicit refusals, reasoning constraints, or fact-check triggers) before forwarding to the LLM. During training, the guardrail generator learns from a mixture of clean and adversarial prompt-response pairs, optimizing to minimize successful jailbreaks while preserving helpfulness.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate DCP on benchmarks including AdvBench, JailbreakBench, and real-world adversarial prompt datasets. Baselines will include standard system prompts, RLHF-tuned models, and existing adversarial prompting defenses. Metrics include attack success rate, helpfulness (via human and automatic scoring), and false positive rate. Ablations will assess the impact of classifier accuracy and guardrail generation strategies."
}
